payload.importance: The answer determines whether certification take-up alone is an adequate comparative-static statistic or whether a regulator or platform must also track the inventory of outstanding certificates, depletion by trade, the resulting uncertified-pool composition, and buyers' private information acquisition. This is an economic identification consequence, not a claimed welfare ranking.
payload.kill_condition: Kill the proposed consumable-stock channel if, over the pre-registered admissible parameter domain and the same equilibrium-selection rule, no Y in {Q,A,U} and no ordered triple k1 < k2 < k3 satisfies the strict sign-reversal test with a nonzero stock-dependent component on at least one adjacent cost interval; also reject a stock attribution whenever every observed reversal is fully reproduced by the flow-certificate/frozen-inspection mechanical benchmark or by the flow-certificate/endogenous-inspection benchmark with zero stock and stock-by-inspection residuals.
payload.object_to_explain: Fix all primitives other than k and one pre-registered equilibrium-selection rule. For each stationary outcome define Q(k) as the probability an arriving buyer pays c to inspect, A(k) as the probability the buyer receives successful service, and U(k) as expected service value minus c times the inspection indicator, with exit normalized to zero. Does there exist an admissible primitive vector and 0 <= k1 < k2 < k3 such that, for at least one Y in {Q,A,U}, (Y(k2)-Y(k1)) times (Y(k3)-Y(k2)) is strictly negative in the full consumable-certificate model, and an exact benchmark decomposition assigns a nonzero component to certificate stock/depletion or its interaction with endogenous inspection?
payload.phenomenon: As truthful certification cost k varies, the endogenous inventory and trade-triggered depletion of visible certificates can change which current capacities remain in the uncertified pool and therefore the return to inspecting one uncertified seller; the proposed object is a possible strict non-monotonicity, with no direction asserted.
prohibited_claims: No direction for Q(k), A(k), or U(k)
No existence or proof of non-monotonicity
No validation of the stock/depletion mechanism
No welfare ranking or policy optimum
No human confirmation of the question, scope, benchmarks, or equilibrium selection
payload.proposed_scope: A discrete-time stationary two-seller game with an infinite sequence of short-lived buyers. Each long-lived seller privately observes a binary capacity each period; capacity follows a fixed two-state Markov law independent across sellers conditional on common parameters. A high-capacity seller with no outstanding certificate may pay k to bank one truthful certified unit of successful service. The binary certificate stock is public, carries over until a buyer trades with that seller, guarantees that trade's success, and is then depleted. An uncertified trade succeeds exactly when current capacity is high. After certification choices and public stock observation, the buyer may pay c to inspect one uncertified seller's current capacity, buy without inspection, or exit. A successful trade gives normalized buyer value one and seller margin m; sellers discount by delta. Outcomes use stationary symmetric Markov-perfect equilibria under a single selection rule fixed before varying k and before applying the benchmark interventions.
payload.unresolved_delta: Whether any fixed admissible primitives and consistently selected stationary equilibrium satisfy the strict sign-reversal test, and if so whether the stock term or stock-by-inspection interaction is necessary after separately measuring the mechanical cheaper-certification and inspection-response terms.

entity_versions.summary: A proposed existence-and-attribution question for stationary inspection, successful allocation, and buyer surplus as certification cost varies.
entity_versions.title: Can consumable certificate stock make lower-cost certification non-monotone?

exact_primitives: Two long-lived sellers and one new short-lived buyer per period
Independent binary seller capacities with one fixed Markov transition matrix
Seller-observed current capacity and publicly observed binary certificate stocks
A high-capacity seller without a certificate may pay k to bank one truthful certified successful service
A certificate carries over until trade with its seller and is depleted by that trade
An uncertified trade succeeds exactly when current capacity is high
Buyer actions are inspect one uncertified seller at cost c, buy without inspection, or exit
Successful service yields buyer value one and seller margin m; seller discount factor is delta
benchmarks.label: F: consumable stock with endogenous inspection
benchmarks.prediction: Compute Q_F(k), A_F(k), and U_F(k) and apply the strict adjacent-difference sign-reversal test; no sign or existence is imposed.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary symmetric Markov-perfect equilibrium with one pre-registered selection rule held fixed for every k and benchmark cell.
timing: Capacities transition and are privately observed by their sellers
Eligible high-capacity sellers simultaneously choose whether to pay k and certify
The buyer observes both certificate stocks
The buyer chooses inspection, uninspected purchase, or exit; an inspection reveals one uncertified seller's current capacity before the purchase decision
Trade and service occur; certified trade succeeds and depletes that certificate, while uncertified trade succeeds only at high current capacity
Payoffs accrue and any unconsumed certificates carry into the next period
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: This cell contains mechanical certification-cost effects, stock/depletion effects, endogenous inspection effects, and their interaction, to be separated by the other three cells.

exact_primitives: All primitives of F except certificate carryover and trade-triggered depletion
A high-capacity seller may pay k for a truthful certificate usable by the current buyer only
An unused certificate expires at the end of its issuance period, so certificate stock is always zero at the next period's start
The buyer still chooses whether to inspect one uncertified seller at cost c
benchmarks.label: N: flow-only certificate with endogenous inspection
benchmarks.prediction: Compute Q_N(k), A_N(k), and U_N(k); relative to the frozen-inspection flow cell, the cost response measures endogenous inspection without a certificate stock.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary symmetric Markov-perfect equilibrium under the same pre-registered selection rule as F, with no certificate-stock state.
timing: Capacities transition and are privately observed
High-capacity sellers may pay k for a current-period certificate
The buyer observes current certificates and endogenously chooses inspection, purchase, or exit
Trade and service occur
Every current certificate expires after this buyer, whether used or unused
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: For any cost interval, Delta Y_N minus Delta Y_NX is the endogenous-inspection component when stock and depletion are absent; its sign is not specified.

exact_primitives: All capacity, certification, carryover, depletion, payoff, and discount primitives of F
Before varying k, fix an anchor cost k_bar and record F's selected stationary state-contingent inspection kernel q_bar
Replace the buyer's inspection choice by q_bar in every state and at every k
Conditional on the imposed inspection realization and signal, the buyer retains the purchase-versus-exit choice
benchmarks.label: X: consumable stock with frozen inspection
benchmarks.prediction: Compute Q_X(k), A_X(k), and U_X(k), with Q_X mechanically fixed by q_bar; relative to NX, the cost response measures the stock/depletion component when inspection does not respond to k.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary symmetric seller Markov-perfect equilibrium with the fixed buyer inspection kernel q_bar and optimal purchase-or-exit behavior conditional on realized information, using the same seller-equilibrium selection rule across k.
timing: Capacities transition and are privately observed
Eligible high-capacity sellers choose certification and pay k
Public certificate stocks are observed
Inspection is drawn from the fixed kernel q_bar rather than re-optimized as k changes
The buyer purchases or exits, trade occurs, and any traded certificate is depleted
Unconsumed certificates carry over
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: For any cost interval, Delta Y_X minus Delta Y_NX is the pure stock/depletion component under frozen inspection; its sign is not specified.

exact_primitives: All primitives of N's flow-only certificate environment
The same anchor inspection kernel q_bar used in X
Inspection is imposed by q_bar for every k and no certificate carries into another period
Conditional on imposed information, the buyer retains the purchase-versus-exit choice
benchmarks.label: NX: flow-only certificate with frozen inspection
benchmarks.prediction: Compute Q_NX(k), A_NX(k), and U_NX(k); each cost response is the mechanical cheaper-certification baseline with neither carryover/depletion nor an endogenous inspection response.
benchmarks.solution_concept: Stationary symmetric seller Markov-perfect equilibrium with q_bar fixed, no certificate-stock state, and the same seller-equilibrium selection rule across k.
timing: Capacities transition and are privately observed
High-capacity sellers may pay k for current-period certification
The buyer observes current certificates
Inspection is drawn from q_bar, followed by purchase or exit
Trade occurs and all certificates expire at period end
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: For any cost interval, Delta Y_NX is the mechanical certification-cost component; its sign is not specified.

payload.exact_question_delta: For each Y in {Q,A,U}, each benchmark B in {F,N,X,NX}, and each lowering from k_H to k_L < k_H, define Delta Y_B = Y_B(k_L)-Y_B(k_H). Decompose Delta Y_F exactly as M_Y + S_Y + I_Y + R_Y, where M_Y = Delta Y_NX is the mechanical cheaper-certification component, S_Y = Delta Y_X-Delta Y_NX is the consumable-stock/depletion component under frozen inspection, I_Y = Delta Y_N-Delta Y_NX is the endogenous-inspection component without stock, and R_Y = Delta Y_F-Delta Y_X-Delta Y_N+Delta Y_NX is the stock-by-inspection interaction. Apply this identity on both adjacent intervals of every candidate k1 < k2 < k3. A strict full-model sign reversal is attributed to the proposed channel only if S_Y or R_Y is nonzero on an interval needed for the reversal; no component's sign is presupposed.

entity_versions.summary: Four exact benchmark cells separate mechanical cheaper certification, certificate stock/depletion, endogenous inspection, and their interaction.
entity_versions.title: Certificate stock by buyer inspection factorial benchmarks

edges.economic_meaning: Each persistent seller privately carries the capacity state that conditions eligibility and service success.
High current capacity is the eligibility condition for truthful issuance.
The perturbed issuance cost enters the seller's certification payoff.
Margin and discounting determine the benefit of creating a current or persistent certificate.
Banked-service semantics make an outstanding certificate a payoff-relevant stock across capacity transitions.
Successful issuance creates the public certificate state subject to cell-specific persistence.
The flow intervention removes cross-period carryover and replaces depletion by period-end expiration.
Issuance, trade, depletion, and carryover order determine the next public stock.
The current stock configuration is the buyer's pre-search public signal.
Certificate availability changes which sellers can be inspected and the outside option to search.
The buyer compares the gross value of information with c.
In X and NX the fixed q_bar kernel replaces endogenous inspection optimization.
A realized inspection produces one private capacity signal.
The revealed capacity enters the final seller-or-exit decision.
Visible certificates provide a certified purchase option before and after any inspection.
Buyer payoffs determine the benefit of information relative to c.
Success value and exit normalization govern final choice.
Choosing a certified seller consumes its certificate and changes the next state in stock cells.
Capacity transitions supply the underlying high-low distribution.
Selective issuance removes some high-capacity seller-periods from the uncertified set.
Certificate survival and depletion determine which seller-periods remain publicly uncertified.
The conditional chance of finding high capacity is a key input to the value of inspection.
Certified purchase options determine the opportunity cost of inspecting an uncertified seller.
In endogenous cells the gross return to information is compared with c.
Selected seller continuation behavior determines issuance at each k.
Selected equilibrium beliefs and choices determine inspection in F and N and seller responses to q_bar in X and NX.
Stationary averaging of the inspection action produces Q(k).
The selected seller or exit determines whether a service attempt contributes to A(k).
Current capacity determines success for uncertified trade.
The banked-service interpretation determines success for certified trade.
Successful-service probability supplies the value component of buyer surplus.
Inspection frequency supplies the expected search-cost component of buyer surplus.
Each component is defined from the response to the same ordered change in k.
The four Q responses generate mechanical, stock, inspection, and interaction contrasts.
The four A responses generate the corresponding exact contrasts.
The four U responses generate the corresponding exact contrasts.
Payoff units and exit origin explain which buyer payoff conventions are normalizations.
Seller relabeling is innocuous, whereas symmetry of selected strategies remains a substantive restriction.

nodes.economic_meaning: Seller j in {1,2} repeatedly observes its capacity, chooses certification when eligible, receives margin from successful trade, and values future profit. This actor set is common to F, N, X, and NX.
nodes.label: Two long-lived sellers
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Exactly two sellers persist across periods
Seller identities are publicly known

nodes.economic_meaning: One fresh buyer acts in each period, receives no continuation payoff, and chooses inspection, purchase, or exit. This actor structure is common to every benchmark.
nodes.label: Sequence of short-lived buyers
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Each buyer participates in one period only
One buyer arrival defines the period time unit

nodes.economic_meaning: Each seller's current capacity theta_jt is high or low, is privately observed by that seller, and follows a fixed two-state Markov transition law. Binary support and conditional independence are substantive scope choices held fixed across all four benchmarks, not consequences of certification.
nodes.label: Binary private capacity process
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The transition matrix is invariant to k and benchmark cell
Current capacities are conditionally independent across sellers under the proposed scope

nodes.economic_meaning: The framing treats a certificate purchased in a high-capacity period as one banked unit that guarantees a successful future certified trade. This gives economic content to persistence when capacity later changes, but the seed also permits a current-state-certification reading; the choice therefore carries a human-owned G1 scope cost.
nodes.label: Truthful banked-service interpretation
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Only a currently high-capacity seller may issue
A certified trade succeeds independently of the seller's contemporaneous uncertified capacity
A certificate is attached to one seller and one eventual trade

nodes.economic_meaning: In F and X, z_jt in {0,1} carries over until trade with seller j and that trade depletes it. In N and NX, issuance is flow-only and every certificate expires after the current buyer, so no stock enters the next period. This is the exact stock intervention, not a normalization.
nodes.label: Public binary certificate stock
primitive_sufficient_conditions: At most one outstanding certificate per seller
Certificate stocks are publicly observed before buyer information acquisition
Trade-triggered depletion applies only in F and X

nodes.economic_meaning: A seller that is currently high and has no outstanding certificate chooses whether to pay k and issue. The action is endogenous in every benchmark; the institutional persistence of its output differs across stock and flow cells.
nodes.label: Seller certification decision
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Low-capacity issuance is infeasible
Certification is truthful by construction
Eligible sellers choose simultaneously

nodes.economic_meaning: k is the sole comparative-static primitive. Every non-k primitive and the pre-registered selection rule are held fixed when comparing ordered costs, so a change in k is not bundled with a technology or information change.
nodes.label: Certification cost k
primitive_sufficient_conditions: k is nonnegative
Cost is paid on issuance rather than on certificate consumption

nodes.economic_meaning: Before choosing search or trade, the buyer observes which sellers currently display certificates but not uncertified capacities. In F and X the display can reflect past issuance and survival; in N and NX it reflects current-period issuance only.
nodes.label: Observed certificate configuration
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Certificate visibility is costless and truthful
Uncertified capacity is not publicly observed

nodes.economic_meaning: In F and N the buyer may pay c to inspect one uncertified seller before purchase. In X and NX this choice is replaced by the same pre-recorded state-contingent kernel q_bar; conditional purchase and exit remain buyer choices. Endogeneity of inspection is therefore the exact second benchmark intervention.
nodes.label: Inspection of one uncertified seller
primitive_sufficient_conditions: At most one uncertified seller is inspected
Inspection precedes the final purchase-or-exit choice
The fixed kernel q_bar is identical in X and NX and invariant to k

nodes.economic_meaning: c is paid exactly when inspection occurs and is held fixed across k and benchmarks. It governs the private return threshold for information acquisition but is not itself the comparative-static perturbation.
nodes.label: Inspection cost c
primitive_sufficient_conditions: c is nonnegative
No inspection cost is paid after exit or an uninspected purchase

nodes.economic_meaning: Inspection reveals the selected uncertified seller's current high-or-low capacity before the buyer's final action. Signal precision is common to all cells in which inspection is realized and is a substantive information primitive.
nodes.label: Perfect capacity inspection signal
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The signal concerns current capacity
The signal is observed only by the inspecting buyer
The signal is perfectly accurate under the proposed scope

nodes.economic_meaning: After public information and any inspection signal, the buyer chooses a seller for one trade, buys from an uncertified seller without further inspection, or exits. This action determines certificate depletion and whether service is attempted.
nodes.label: Purchase or exit decision
primitive_sufficient_conditions: At most one seller trades with the current buyer
Exit yields no service and no trade payoff

nodes.economic_meaning: Capacity transition and private observation precede certification; public certificate observation precedes inspection; inspection precedes purchase or exit; trade precedes stock carryover. This order is common to all cells except that flow certificates expire at the period boundary in N and NX.
nodes.label: Within-period event order
primitive_sufficient_conditions: No seller changes certification after observing buyer inspection
No second buyer arrives before depletion or expiration is applied

nodes.economic_meaning: Buyer payoff is normalized to one for successful service, zero for failure or exit, minus c if inspection occurs. Because the seed does not specify a purchase price, this treats any purchase transfer as fixed and absorbed into the value normalization; allowing endogenous prices would be a substantive extension, not part of the current question.
nodes.label: Buyer payoff and transfer normalization
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Successful-service net value is normalized to one
Purchase transfers do not vary with k, stock, or inspection
Exit payoff is normalized to zero

nodes.economic_meaning: A seller earns fixed margin m on successful trade, pays k when issuing, and discounts future profit by delta. Failure produces no margin under the proposed scope. The scale of m is a payoff-unit normalization only if ratios k/m and buyer-side primitives are preserved.
nodes.label: Seller margin and discounting
primitive_sufficient_conditions: m is positive and fixed across benchmarks
delta lies strictly between zero and one
Certification cost is borne by the issuing seller

nodes.economic_meaning: The proposed solution concept selects stationary symmetric Markov-perfect behavior for sellers and endogenous buyers, with seller equilibrium under the imposed q_bar kernels in X and NX. Restricting to symmetry and fixing a cross-k, cross-cell selection rule are substantive scope choices needed to distinguish true non-monotonicity from equilibrium switching.
nodes.label: Stationary symmetric Markov equilibrium and selection
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Strategies condition only on payoff-relevant current public state and each seller's private capacity
One selection rule is declared before scanning k
The rule is mapped consistently to each benchmark intervention

nodes.economic_meaning: Seller labels are exchangeable, successful-service buyer value is the value unit, exit is the payoff origin, and one buyer arrival is the time unit. These are intended normalizations. Binary capacity, certificate persistence, perfect inspection, symmetric-equilibrium restriction, and fixed pricing are not relabelings and remain scope choices.
nodes.label: Pure labels and units
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Relabeling sellers leaves primitives unchanged
All costs and margins are rescaled with the chosen payoff unit
Changing time units preserves the capacity transition and discount mapping

nodes.economic_meaning: Moving from F to N or from X to NX replaces carryover and trade-triggered depletion by end-of-period expiration while leaving eligibility, truthfulness, k, capacity, payoffs, and the relevant inspection regime fixed.
nodes.label: No-stock flow-certificate intervention
primitive_sufficient_conditions: No certificate survives the issuance period
The intervention does not directly change k or c

nodes.economic_meaning: Moving from F to X or from N to NX replaces buyer optimization over inspection by the identical q_bar kernel recorded at a pre-specified anchor k_bar, holding stock or flow certification fixed. The anchor and off-path state support must be fixed before comparison.
nodes.label: Frozen-inspection intervention
primitive_sufficient_conditions: q_bar is defined for every public state reached in both stock and flow cells
q_bar does not change with k
Purchase and exit remain responsive to realized information

nodes.economic_meaning: The stationary distribution of current capacity conditional on a seller displaying no certificate is jointly induced by capacity transitions, selective issuance, certificate survival, and trade depletion. It is a derived equilibrium composition, not an exogenous quality distribution.
nodes.label: Composition of the uncertified pool
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Compute separately for each k and benchmark cell
Condition on the public state used by the buyer

nodes.economic_meaning: The value of inspecting one uncertified seller is the buyer's optimal expected payoff with the signal minus the best payoff without inspection, before subtracting c. It is derived from uncertified-pool beliefs and available certified alternatives and may feed back into depletion through buyer choice.
nodes.label: Private return to inspection
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Evaluate under the same public state and continuation-free buyer problem
Do not infer a sign as k changes

nodes.economic_meaning: Q(k) is the stationary probability that an arriving buyer pays c and inspects. In X and NX it is generated mechanically by q_bar; in F and N it is an equilibrium choice probability.
nodes.label: Stationary inspection rate Q(k)
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Average over the selected stationary public-state distribution
Count at most one inspection per buyer

nodes.economic_meaning: A(k) is the stationary probability that an arriving buyer receives successful service, counting exit and failed uncertified trade as zero. It is a gross allocation-success measure, not a net welfare claim.
nodes.label: Successful-allocation rate A(k)
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Certified service succeeds under the banked-service interpretation
Uncertified service succeeds exactly at high current capacity

nodes.economic_meaning: U(k) is stationary expected successful-service value minus c times the inspection indicator under the fixed-transfer normalization. It is buyer surplus in the proposed scope, not total surplus and not a welfare ranking.
nodes.label: Buyer surplus U(k)
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Use the buyer payoff normalization node
Average over buyers before conditioning on trade

nodes.economic_meaning: For each Y and cost interval, Delta Y_F is decomposed into M=Delta Y_NX, S=Delta Y_X-Delta Y_NX, I=Delta Y_N-Delta Y_NX, and R=Delta Y_F-Delta Y_X-Delta Y_N+Delta Y_NX. These are exact accounting contrasts; none is assigned a sign or causal importance in advance.
nodes.label: Four-cell cost-response decomposition
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Use identical non-intervened primitives in all four cells
Apply the same equilibrium-selection rule
Evaluate both adjacent intervals of each ordered k triple

entity_versions.summary: A proposal-only decomposition of actors, capacity, certification stock, information, actions, timing, payoffs, benchmark interventions, equilibrium objects, and measured outcomes.
entity_versions.title: Primitive graph for consumable quality certificates

payload.rationale: Recommend human G1 approval of the question and four-cell benchmark architecture for formal development only if the human explicitly accepts the banked-service certificate semantics, fixed-transfer buyer-surplus normalization, stationary symmetric solution scope, common selection discipline, and off-support definition of q_bar. Otherwise revise or narrow at G1. This recommendation neither confirms the gate nor asserts a result, direction, novelty, validation, or welfare conclusion.

requirements.description: The question fixes Q(k), A(k), U(k), a strict adjacent-difference sign-reversal test, non-k primitives, and a common equilibrium-selection rule.

The economic consequence is whether certification take-up suffices for comparative-static diagnosis or stock, depletion, uncertified-pool composition, and private inspection must also be tracked; no welfare claim is made.

The channel is killed if the pre-registered domain and selection yield no strict reversal requiring a nonzero stock or stock-by-inspection component, and stock attribution is rejected when flow benchmarks fully reproduce any reversal.
F, N, X, and NX cross consumable versus flow certification with endogenous versus frozen inspection, generating exact mechanical, stock, inspection, and interaction cost-response contrasts.

Actors, capacity, certificate semantics and stock, timing, public and inspected information, seller and buyer actions, payoffs, solution concept, benchmark interventions, derived pool composition, inspection return, and measured outcomes are explicitly separated.

Human review must accept, revise, or narrow the banked-successful-service interpretation; treating a persistent certificate as information only about issuance-period capacity would define a different dynamic environment.

Symmetric stationary Markov equilibrium and the common cross-k, cross-cell selection rule exclude apparent reversals caused solely by untracked equilibrium switching, but they are substantive restrictions needing human scope ownership.
The frozen kernel q_bar needs a human-accepted anchor k_bar and a rule on public states outside the anchor equilibrium support so X and NX remain exact benchmarks.
Buyer surplus currently normalizes purchase transfers as fixed; endogenous prices or margins would be an extension and require reframing before formal work.
No human G1 question-and-benchmark decision is recorded. The dossier and its approve recommendation are proposals only and cannot promote the scope.

entity_versions.summary: An agent-prepared dossier for human review of the exact question, four benchmark cells, primitive decomposition, kill condition, and disclosed scope risks.
entity_versions.title: G1 dossier: consumable certificate question and benchmarks

route_outcomes.rationale: Proposes exactly one falsifiable ResearchQuestion and one four-cell BenchmarkSet. One frames relation binds the set to the question, and one exact benchmark_delta relation binds each benchmark record to the same ResearchQuestion. All content remains agent-proposed and unvalidated pending a later human G1 decision.

Creates one PrimitiveGraph tied to the exact ResearchQuestion and BenchmarkSet and one proposal-only G1 GateDossier whose ordered objects include the exact ResearchQuestion. Exact decomposes, governs, and supports relations keep both outputs in that scope. No G1 decision or human approval is recorded.
